options: To avoid plagiarism and give credit to the original author
To show you have read many books
questions.explanation: The module: 'Whenever you quote someone’s words or use someone’s work, you need to acknowledge the person so that you do not pass the work off as your own.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is intellectual property?
options: A product someone creates based on his or her thoughts or ideas
questions.explanation: The module mentions: 'you are stealing someone’s intellectual property' when plagiarizing. (Unit 5)

questions.question: Where does a bibliography appear in a paper?
options: At the end of the essay
questions.explanation: The module: 'it comes at the end of the essay.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What are the three factors that determine which documentation style to use?
options: Course requirements, discipline standards, individual preference
Book type, journal type, website type
questions.explanation: The module: 'Choosing the appropriate documentation style for your paper may depend on three factors: • The requirements of the particular course; • The standard for the discipline; or • Your individual preference.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a works cited list?
options: A list of sources cited in the paper
A list of all sources read
A list of sources not cited
questions.explanation: The module uses 'works cited' interchangeably with bibliography, but it generally means list of sources cited. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What should you do if you are not sure which documentation style to use?
questions.explanation: The module: 'If no documentation style is prescribed, you should ask whether the instructor has a preference.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the purpose of providing a bibliography?
options: To allow the reader to locate the sources you have used
To make the paper look professional
questions.explanation: The module: 'Citing sources not only gives credit where it’s due but also allows your reader to locate the sources you have consulted.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What should you include in your notes when researching?
options: Complete bibliographic information and page numbers
questions.explanation: The module: 'Write down the most complete bibliographic information available for each source... note the number of the page(s).' (Unit 5)

questions.question: Why should you put citations in your paper as you draft?
options: To ensure you don't forget where information came from
To save time at the end
questions.explanation: The module recommends putting citations as you draft to keep track. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is academic dishonesty?
options: Plagiarism, such as using someone else's work without acknowledgement
questions.explanation: The module: 'you are engaging in academic dishonesty, known as plagiarism.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the main purpose of documentation styles?
options: To standardize how sources are cited and formatted
questions.explanation: The module: 'A documentation style is a standard approach to the citation of sources... It prescribes methods for citing references...' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a source?
options: Any book, article, website, or other material consulted for research
questions.explanation: The module refers to 'sources' broadly, including books, articles, etc. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the difference between a paraphrase and a summary?
options: A paraphrase restates in your own words; a summary condenses the main points
questions.explanation: The module lists both as needing documentation, and earlier defined summary as a brief paraphrase. (Unit 3 and Unit 5)

questions.question: Why is it important to document paraphrases?
options: Because the idea originates from someone else, even if the words are your own
Because it makes the paper longer
questions.explanation: The module: 'Paraphrases and summaries' must be documented. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a bibliographic entry?
options: A single record in a bibliography listing a source
questions.explanation: The module shows an example of a bibliographic entry: 'Smith, Jacob. Understanding Reading. Malta: Springer Publishers, 2003.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the order of elements in a bibliographic entry according to the module?
questions.explanation: The example: 'Smith, Jacob. Understanding Reading. Malta: Springer Publishers, 2003.' – Author, Title, Place: Publisher, Date. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a citation style?
options: A set of rules for formatting citations and references
questions.explanation: The module refers to styles like MLA, APA, Chicago – these are sets of rules. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the role of the instructor in choosing a documentation style?
options: The instructor may assign a style or express a preference
questions.explanation: The module: 'Your instructor may assign a documentation style... If no documentation style is prescribed, you should ask whether the instructor has a preference.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a standard documentation style?
options: It provides consistency and helps readers locate sources
questions.explanation: The module implies that standard styles help in citation and location of sources. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a reference?
options: A citation to a source within the text or in a list
questions.explanation: The module uses 'reference' to mean citation. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the difference between a bibliography and a reference list?
options: A bibliography includes all sources consulted; a reference list includes only those cited
A reference list is for books only
A bibliography is for articles only
questions.explanation: The module defines bibliography as 'a list of all of the sources you have used (whether referenced or not)', implying a reference list is only those cited. (Unit 5)

questions.question: Why should you not plagiarize?
options: It is academic dishonesty and steals someone's intellectual property
questions.explanation: The module: 'you are engaging in academic dishonesty, known as plagiarism and you are stealing someone’s intellectual property.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What should you do if you are unsure whether information is common knowledge?
questions.explanation: The module implies that if it's not common knowledge, you must cite it. When in doubt, cite. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the purpose of the working bibliography?
options: To keep a running list of sources as you draft
To list all books in the library
questions.explanation: The module: 'add the source to your working bibliography' when you place a citation. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What should you do when you have finished drafting your paper?
options: Review your citations and bibliography for accuracy and proper format
questions.explanation: The module suggests that when it comes time to put finishing touches, you can easily format citations from your working notes. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the benefit of keeping good notes during research?
options: It makes applying documentation styles easier
questions.explanation: The module: 'You can make the process of applying any documentation style easier if you keep good notes while you perform research.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the difference between a direct quotation and a paraphrase?
options: Direct quotation uses the exact words; paraphrase uses your own words
questions.explanation: The module distinguishes them as needing documentation. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the importance of page numbers in citations?
options: To help the reader locate the exact source of the quotation or idea
To show you read the book
questions.explanation: The module: 'note the number of the page(s) on which the information appears.' (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is a citation?
options: A reference to a source, usually including author, title, and publication details
questions.explanation: The module uses 'citation' as a reference to a source. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the purpose of a works cited list?
options: To list only sources that were cited in the paper
questions.explanation: The module uses 'works cited' interchangeably with bibliography, but generally it is a list of cited sources. (Unit 5)

questions.question: What is the first step in creating a bibliography?
options: Gather complete bibliographic information for each source used
questions.explanation: The module: 'Write down the most complete bibliographic information available for each source.' (Unit 5)
